data.title: Vertiseit publishes the Annual Report for 2025
data.description:
Vertiseit AB's Annual Report for 2025 is now available on the company's websitevertiseit.com

Links

Link to Vertiseit Investor Relations where the report is available

Contacts

Johan Lind, Vertiseit CEO/ Media Contact

+46 703 579 154

Jonas Lagerqvist, Vertiseit Deputy CEO / CFO / Investor Relations

jonas.lagerqvist@vertiseit.com

+46 732 036 298

Redeye Nordic Growth AB is the company’s Certified Adviser

About Vertiseit

Vertiseit is a leading platform company within In-Store Experience Management (IXM). The company operates through its subidiaries Dise, Grassfish and Visual Art that enable global brands and leading retailers to strengthen the customer experience by offering a seamless customer journey through connecting the physical and digital meeting. The company has around 270 employees in Sweden, Norway, Denmark, Finland, Austria, Germany, Spain, UK and USA. During the period 2012-2025, Vertiseit performed an average profitable growth of recurring SaaS revenue (ARR) of 48 percent (CAGR). For the full year of 2025, the group’s net revenue amounted to SEK 676 million, with an adjusted EBITDA margin of 16 percent. Since 2019, Vertiseit’s B-share is listed on Nasdaq First North Growth Market.

data.title: Nike Is Trading at 8 Year Low Ahead of Earnings. Why Analysts Call It a Buy. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Kit Norton

Nike fiscal third-quarter earnings are due after the closing bell Tuesday and management will be seeking to convince investors that the company's business turnaround is the real deal.

Wall Street expects the Beaverton, Ore.-based footwear and athletic apparel retailer to post fiscal third-quarter earnings of 29 cents per share, representing a 46.3% drop-off compared with a year ago, with revenue totaling $11.23 billion, which would mark a 0.3% decline year-over-year, according to FactSet.

A team of Jefferies analysts, led by Randal Konik, on Monday wrote they expect fiscal third-quarter performance to be driven by sales in North America and in Nike's wholesale segment. However, the analysts believe the China market and direct-to-consumer sales "remain under pressure."

The firm added that Nike is progressing in turning around its business and that momentum is building in North America.

The third-quarter earnings results come at 4:15 p.m. Eastern time on Tuesday. Company executives are scheduled to hold the conference call at 5 p.m. ET.

During the conference call, investors are expected to be looking to see if the company changes course and decides to provide full-year guidance. Nike has refrained from offering a full-year outlook for several quarters now as it works on its business turnaround.

Recent investor focus has been on Nike leadership and CEO Elliott Hill, who took the job in October 2024, on improving the company's business direction and on maintaining stable growth.

However, the market has been unconvinced thus far by Hill and Nike.

Nike stock advanced 1.7% to $52.10 on Tuesday ahead of the earnings report. For comparison, the S&P 500 and the Dow Jones Industrial Average gained 1.3% and 0.9%, respectively.

On Monday, Nike closed at $50.98, it's lowest closing price since Oct. 13, 2017, according to Dow Jones Market Data. Entering Tuesday, Nike shares were down 17.59% in March, part of a 19.57% decline on the year.

In December 2025, Nike surpassed Wall Street predictions for both fiscal second-quarter earnings and revenue, but investors were disappointed over declining profits and lackluster sales in China.

Nike's third-quarter guidance also did not sit well with investors, with the footwear and athletic apparel retailer forecasting modest growth in North America with trends in Greater China and Converse in line with the second quarter.

Nike also expects gross margins to be down between 1.75 and 2.25 percentage points from a year ago.

"Over the past several quarters, Nike has struggled significantly, amid a confluence of unfavorable external and internal hindrances, which have weighed meaningfully upon sales growth and profit expansion," Oppenheimer analysts Brian Nagel and Andrew Chasanoff wrote Friday.

The analysts added that challenges persist for Nike but the current share price indicates "downbeat market sentiment," which could lead to "favorable re-rating as fundamental trends at the company re-strengthen and start to consistently top Street forecasts."

Oppenheimer has a price target of $120 on Nike, along with a "top pick" designation in its "consumer growth & ecommerce" coverage group. The firm has an Outperform rating on Nike.

It appears Nike's turnaround is far from over. In a March 5 regulatory filing, Nike said it expects to take a pretax charge of about $300 million, mostly in the third quarter, because of employee severance costs over a nine month period ending Feb. 28. The company said the move comes as management continues to evaluate opportunities to operate more efficiently and profitably.

However, the Jefferies analysts wrote that Nike's business is tracking in the right direction and that the upcoming FIFA World Cup is a clear global catalyst.

"We advise buying shares now as the turnaround advances," Konik wrote. Jefferies has a Buy rating on the stock with a $110 price target.

Write to Kit Norton at kit.norton@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: KKR Plans to Offer to Acquire Taiyo Holdings
data.description:
By Nicholas G. Miller

KKR intends to make a tender offer to acquire Japanese electronic materials manufacturer Taiyo Holdings for 4,750 Japanese Yen (US$29.88) a share.

The price represents a 117% premium to the six-month average unaffected closing price of Taiyo shares as of May 27, 2025.

Taiyo said its board will support the offer. Taiyo investors DIC and Kowa have also each agreed to sell their shares to Taiyo Holdings through a share consolidation and buyback. Meanwhile, Oasis Management agreed to tender its shares representing about 16% of Taiyo outstanding shares.

With these agreements, KKR has secured support for the deal from shareholders representing about 42% of the company's outstanding shares.

Following the privatization, Taiyo Holdings' founding family plans to re-invest in KJ005HD, the KKR-managed investment vehicle that will own Taiyo Holdings.

"As a private company, we will be able to pursue long-term investments in our core technologies with greater focus and stability, which we could not achieve on our own," Taiyo Chief Executive Hitoshi Saito said.

Write to Nicholas G. Miller at nicholas.miller@wsj.com.

data.title: Australia Probes Meta, Snap, TikTok Over Under-16 Ban, Flags A$49.5M Risk
data.description:
Regulatory pressure on social media platforms could be moving into a more consequential phase, as Australia's online safety watchdog begins its first formal compliance review into how major platforms are enforcing a nationwide ban on users under 16. The probe targets Meta Platforms , Snap , TikTok and YouTube, with the regulator flagging significant concerns around enforcement gaps just months after the law took effect on Dec. 10, 2025. Early findings suggest safeguards may not be robust enough to stop underage users from creating accounts, with some systems allowing repeated attempts at age verification until a user succeeds, raising questions about how effectively these platforms are policing access.

The regulator's initial assessment could carry both financial and reputational implications, with potential penalties reaching as high as A$49.5 million ($34 million) if companies are found not to have taken reasonable steps to comply. Authorities have started gathering evidence and could decide on enforcement actions by mid-year, indicating that this review may evolve into a broader regulatory test case. While officials noted there are fewer under-16 users compared with four months ago, they also indicated that significant numbers of underage users remain active, suggesting compliance measures may still be falling short of expectations.

The timing adds another layer of pressure, coming shortly after Meta and Alphabet Inc. were found liable in a US case tied to a 20-year-old woman's mental health struggles linked to social media use, with damages awarded. That ruling has raised comparisons to a potential Big Tobacco moment for the sector, as legal and regulatory scrutiny appears to be building. Regulators argue platforms have the capability to comply today, while companies such as Meta have said that accurately determining user age remains a challenge across the industry, leaving investors weighing a potentially shifting risk profile if enforcement intensifies.

data.title: Fractile Seeks $200 Million as Nvidia Faces Inference Pressure
data.description:
Fractile, a UK AI chip start-up, is testing investor appetite for alternatives to Nvidia's dominance. The company is in talks to raise more than $200 million at a roughly $1 billion valuation. The round, which hasn't yet been finalized, would include venture capital firm Accel and existing backer Oxford Science Enterprises. It comes after UK rival Olix raised $220 million at a similar valuation, signaling growing investor interest in British inference-focused chip companies. Prior to these talks, Fractile had raised about $15 million in seed financing.

Fractile's architecture uses SRAM co-located with compute to reduce latency and improve efficiency, targeting inference where traditional GPU setups can become constrained. That positioning mirrors a broader industry push, including Groq's approach and Nvidia's own response with new inference-focused chips unveiled at GTC.

The team includes veterans from Nvidia, Imagination Technologies, and Graphcore, the latter acquired for just over $600 million in 2024. Fractile is also leaning into sovereign AI demand, with a 100 million UK expansion plan. If the round closes near target, it would mark a step change in scale and signal that capital is increasingly backing inference as the next battleground.

data.title: SEC Highlights Financial Independence During Financial Literacy Month
data.description:
Washington, D.C.--(Newsfile Corp. - March 31, 2026) - The Securities and Exchange Commission’s Office of Investor Education and Assistance (OIEA) today announced that as part of April’s National Financial Literacy Month it will highlight financial planning tools and resources on Investor.gov to encourage people to build toward financial independence.

Throughout April, SEC staff will provide information to help investors as they create a plan to reach their financial goals and pursue their version of financial independence. Key considerations include starting early; living within your means; investing consistently in a long-term, diversified, risk-appropriate plan; paying down high-interest debt; and having an emergency fund.

"In the spirit of ​America's 250th anniversary, I encourage individuals and families to reflect on the pivotal role that investing can play in achieving their own financial independence," said SEC Chairman Paul S. Atkins. "America's capital markets are the envy of the world, and engaging with them in a risk-appropriate manner can be foundational for a strong financial future."

Staff will also provide investors with information about the benefits of long-term investing using tax-advantaged accounts, building wealth, the power of compound growth, avoiding scams and planning for retirement.

"Investing early helps Americans build wealth for a strong financial future," said John Moses, Acting Director of the SEC's OIEA. “A great way to get started is to create a long-term, diversified plan that helps investors reach their goals. Building wealth slowly by regularly setting money aside for investments helps investors benefit from compound growth, which can drive life-changing results over time.”

OIEA issued an investor bulletin “Investor.gov Tips for 2026” that highlights important information on Investor.gov to help investors make informed investment decisions and avoid investment fraud. Investors can also test their investing knowledge by taking April’s Financial Literacy Month Quiz.

SEC outreach events in April include presentations to military service members and veterans; webinars and events providing investor education and fraud prevention information to older adults; and financial education activities for teachers and students. For more information, visit Investor.gov.

data.title: CoreWeave Closes $8.5B Loan Facility for Cloud Platform Expansion
data.description:
By Katherine Hamilton

CoreWeave closed a $8.5 billion delayed draw term loan facility to support expanding its artificial intelligence cloud platform.

The deal enables the cloud services provider to borrow up to $7.5 billion initially, with the ability to increase total borrowing capacity to $8.5 billion as underlying assets reach stabilization, the company said Tuesday.

The facility provides CoreWeave with more access to low-cost capital. It fulfills financing requirements to deliver previously contracted cloud services with leading AI enterprises, expanding CoreWeave's high-performance AI cloud footprint.

The facility received an A3 rating by Moody's and an A (low) rating by DBRS.

MUFG and Morgan Stanley were co-structuring agents and joint bookrunners with Goldman Sachs and JPMorgan serving as additional coordinating lead arrangers for the deal. The facility was anchored by Blackstone Credit & Insurance.

Write to Katherine Hamilton at katherine.hamilton@wsj.com

data.title: Suncor Energy Increasing Buybacks, Production as Part of Three-Year Plan
data.description:
By Adriano Marchese

Suncor Energy is boosting its share repurchases by more than 20% as part of its thee-year improvement plan, which includes increasing production and refining capacity.

The Calgary, Alberta-based energy company said Tuesday that the increase in its buyback plan brings the amount it will spend up to 4 billion Canadian dollars, equivalent to $2.87 billion.

Suncor is guiding for a C$2 billion increase in normalized free funds flow by 2028 with upstream production reaching 100,000 barrels a day. At the same time, the company expects production costs per barrel to fall. By 2028, Suncor aims to cut its corporate WTI break-even point by $5 a barrel to $38 per barrel.

Refining is also expected to rise, with the company expecting a 10% increase in refining network nameplate capacity to 511,000 barrels per day.

The company's targets come as prices have been increasing, with oil currently trading at more than $100 a barrel, due to the disruption of supplies coming from the Middle East as a result of the war with Iran.

The company now estimates that its contingent resources total 30 billion barrels with no exploration risk. Contingent resources aren't yet classified as reserves because they still require commercial or regulatory steps before development.

Suncor said it has identified projects that could add 400,000 barrels a day in future production at a cost per additional barrel of output of about $30,000 per flowing barrel.

Write to Adriano Marchese at adriano.marchese@wsj.com

data.title: The Financial Planning Expert Who's Boycotting 529s for His Kids — WSJ
data.description:
By Dalvin Brown and Oyin Adedoyin | Photography by Michael Swensen for WSJ

David Blanchett has spent his career studying how Americans save for major life events. But the money for his own four children to attend college won't be coming from college-savings plans.

529 accounts allow families to invest money that they can pull out tax-free to spend on college and other educational expenses. Blanchett, 44 years old, instead keeps his family's investments in accounts that will incur capital-gains taxes when he sells.

What his family loses in tax benefits they gain in flexibility. Blanchett, who takes the lead on his family's investment decisions, could sell to pay tuition for his four children. The oldest is five years from college. He could sell to fund his wife Sarah Blanchett's future veterinary practice. He could sell to pay down the mortgage on their home in Lexington, Ky.

"We are actively saving money that we may use for college," he said. "But not necessarily."

The idea of an account built primarily around higher education is falling flat for some parents. They don't want to tie up their money, especially for a degree that seems increasingly precarious. The payoff is less certain when college expenses are rising and artificial intelligence threatens to upend many professions.

This hesitancy is a counterpoint to the flood of money going into 529s. Total contributions last year increased by about 8% from the year before, according to ISS Market Intelligence. The number of accounts and total assets in them have continued to grow.

But parents are eyeing a widening array of costs competing for their savings. Nearly three-quarters of parents with kids at home say they would consider or have already started planning to help their children buy a house, according to a Northwestern Mutual survey. Of those, 84% rank that as important as paying for college, or more so.

Among parents who aren't saving for college in a 529, 18% said it was because of concerns about higher education's future, according to a Credit Karma survey from last year. Some 13% had doubts about the postgrad job market.

It is increasingly common for families to financially support their kids well into adulthood. Some help with rent and day-to-day bills. Others are preparing to save for their own kids' retirement. And then there are nearer-term costs like funding the parents' retirement.

Families are planning in a far less predictable environment than earlier generations did, said Susan Wachter, a professor of real estate and finance at the Wharton School. Saving for a four-year degree once felt like the obvious way to help a child get ahead. "That's in question," she said.

Blanchett is the head of retirement research for Prudential Financial, where he is also a portfolio manager. He publishes papers with titles like: "Breakeven Returns for Delayed Social Security Claiming." He is widely quoted about tax-advantaged 401(k) accounts, including in The Wall Street Journal.

When it comes to saving in 529s, he is influenced more by his own experience. He and his wife had to pay off more than $400,000 in student loans after graduate school. By the time they started saving, their oldest was close enough to college that the tax advantages of a 529 no longer seemed worth the trade-off. Locking money into a restricted account felt like voluntarily returning to the kind of financial straitjacket they just escaped, he said.

He also sees college and retirement as fundamentally different goals. Retirement savings have decades to compound and may need to support him and his wife for 30 years or more, making the tax break more valuable. College is a shorter-term expense, and one his children could help shoulder. He and his wife have been saving about $100,000 a year into retirement accounts.

He wants to help his children, he said, but not in a way that assumes a four-year degree is the only path.

"The best thing we can do for our kids is to be very stable financially and have lots of emergency savings," he said.

Blanchett knows this is a personal decision, and that his strategy isn't for everyone. People who disagree with him include his identical twin brother.

Brian Blanchett has put nearly $700,000 into 529 plans for his three young children. The Blanchetts's parents were public-school teachers with limited savings for college. He never wanted cost to determine what his children could do.

Brian made contributions from bonuses and stock sales. "Sometimes cash was quite tight," he said. "But I liked the tax efficiency."

Brian works for a biotech company in California and learned only recently that his brother had nothing in a 529. "He's the one with a Ph.D. in financial planning," he said.

In recent years, the allowable uses of 529s have expanded beyond higher education. They can be used to cover room and board, trade schools, professional licensing, K-12 education and student-loan repayment. Up to $35,000 of 529 money can also be rolled into a Roth IRA, subject to limitations. Tax benefits vary by state.

Families have been upping their 529 contributions in part because of the additional uses, said Paul Curley, director of savings research at ISS Market Intelligence. Even so, 529s are meant to be limited to educational uses. Spend the funds on something else and the earnings generally face income tax and a 10% federal penalty.

Two House bills introduced this year would expand 529s to cover some first-time home purchases, though neither has advanced out of the Ways and Means Committee.

For Lauren Ziminsky, a 42-year-old Los Angeles mother of two, a house substitutes for much of her college-savings plan. Ziminsky, a real-estate investor, said her in-laws opened 529s for the children and continue to fund them. But she took $130,000 she might have invested in the accounts and put it down on a $308,000 rental property.

By 2035, when her oldest is college-age, she expects the property will have appreciated in value, and she can do a cash-out refinance to get the money to pay for tuition. If her daughters don't go to college, the money can go toward something else. She posts about the strategy on social media.

"I don't know in 10 years what college is going to mean," she said.

Ziminsky said her thinking on 529s changed when her mother died six months into retirement, after a lifetime of saving in accounts she barely touched.

David Blanchett said he has thought about opening a 529. He compared tax-free growth in a 529 to what his taxable account may earn over the years before his children go to college. The difference is about 3%. Had the family started saving earlier, he said, the answer might be different.

"The most important thing to do is to save money," he said. "How you save it isn't typically going to be as impactful."

Write to Dalvin Brown at dalvin.brown@wsj.com and Oyin Adedoyin at oyin.adedoyin@wsj.com

data.title: Oil Supply Deficit Seen Persisting Through April Even If Iran War Ends — Market Talk
data.description: A global oil supply deficit of around 8.75 million barrels a day is expected to persist through April even if the conflict ends by mid-month, according to Societe Generale. "Gulf producers must restore output--potentially back to pre-conflict levels--tankers must successfully move those barrels to global refining hubs, and, critically, refiners must be able to process the crude into usable products," commodity strategists at the bank say. Restarting production is particularly complex, requiring safety checks, system repairs and gradual well ramp-ups to avoid reservoir damage. Critical infrastructure such as compressors and treatment plants must also be brought back online, a process industry estimates suggest could take weeks to several months, Societe Generale says. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

data.title: CoreWeave Closes $8.5 Billion GPU Loan Backed by Meta
data.description:
CoreWeave rose 6.58% intraday after securing an $8.5 billion loan backed by a combination of GPUs and a Meta Platforms customer contract worth at least $19 billion, in what it says is the largest chip-backed debt deal completed to date. The facility allows CoreWeave to draw up to $7.5 billion initially, rising to the full $8.5 billion once the underlying chips are operational. It matures in March 2032 and was oversubscribed. The deal was led by Mitsubishi UFJ and Morgan Stanley , with Goldman Sachs , JPMorgan , and Blackstone also participating.

By securing debt against GPUs and long-term contracts, CoreWeave can access lower borrowing costs and a broader investor base. The loan carries an investment-grade A3 rating, with pricing around 5.9% for fixed-rate debt and a spread of 2.25 percentage points over SOFR for floating-rate tranches. That is roughly 7.5 percentage points cheaper than similar financing the company obtained in 2023.

The deal also highlights the scale of capital required to support AI demand, with estimates for infrastructure spending exceeding $3 trillion. CoreWeave already held $21.6 billion in debt at the end of last year, alongside $3.7 billion in unused capacity, raising questions about leverage even as financing costs improve.

data.title: Societe Generale Forecasts Brent at $125 Next Month — Market Talk
data.description: Societe Generale forecasts Brent crude at an average of around $125 a barrel in April, with prices potentially spiking to $150 a barrel as nearly 15 million barrels a day of Gulf supply remain offline. "The market is structurally tighter, more fragile, and highly sensitive to further shocks," commodity strategists say. "Prices could go considerably higher if Bab al-Mandeb at the southern end of the Red Sea is effectively shut." The bank now sees Brent at $80 a barrel by the end of the year, up from $65 a barrel previously. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

data.title: Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac Log Biggest Gains in a Decade. There's Still No Decision on Privatization. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Shaina Mishkin

Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac stock on Monday notched their largest one-day increases in over a decade after traders took investor Bill Ackman's advice.

Ackman, founder of the hedge fund Pershing Square Capital Management, said on the social-media website X on Sunday that "some of the highest quality businesses in the world are trading at extremely cheap prices."

"And Fannie and Freddie are stupidly cheap. Asymmetry at its best," he wrote in a follow-up post. "They could be a 10X and it could happen soon."

The stocks took off on Monday: Fannie Mae closed 51% higher, logging its largest one-day gain since August 2009, according to Dow Jones Market Data. Freddie Mac gained 47%, its largest gain since March 2013. Both were rising in Tuesday trading.

It was only the latest fluctuation as investors await signs that the Trump administration will work to remove the companies from their long-running conservatorship or sell shares in a new offering. Despite the one-day increase, both are down this year, and are roughly 50% lower than their 52-week highs in September.

"The last 24 hours, we think, is an Ackman-driven push," says Eric Hagen, who covers the stocks for BTIG. "I wouldn't point to anything really fundamental."

Ackman's hedge fund has large positions in both companies' common shares, according to FactSet. The investor's post on X follows a Barron's report earlier this month that he recently met with Trump administration officials to advocate for retiring the companies' "senior" preferred shares, which represent some of the government's claim on the companies.

There doesn't appear to be any news on administrative action that changes the prospects for Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac investors. The Federal Housing Finance Agency, the White House, Fannie Mae, Freddie Mac didn't immediately respond to Barron's request for comment. Pershing Square declined to comment.

Hagen, the BTIG analyst, values the stocks at $20 each. Fannie Mae is at $7.88 in Tuesday trading. Freddie Mac is at $6.92.

His price target hinges on political action. "We agree with Bill Ackman and we buy into the same argument that some simple procedural changes regarding the senior preferred stock and the level of capital — the capital ratios — would clear the way for a total rerating in the stocks, " Hagen says. "The likelihood of that happening — it changes every day."

Write to Shaina Mishkin at shaina.mishkin@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.
